text: Andi.
Hi, Nathalie.
How are you?
I'm good, thanks.
I appreciate your patience.
I was having issues with my webcam, so.
Oh, no worries.
I know how that goes.
This meeting is being recorded.
All good.
How are you doing?
How's the rest of your day going?
It's going well.
It's still pretty early in my day.
I'm based in Los Angeles, so.
Awesome.
Where are you based?
I am on the East Coast.
I'm in Rhode Island.
Oh.
Okay, so you're pretty far into your day already.
Cool.
Well, since we're five minutes in, I'll just jump in.
I'll give you a bit of background on myself and the role, growthx.
And then I will ask you questions about your background, starting with a general overview.
And then I'll try and leave, like, five to ten minutes at the end for you to ask me any questions.
That come up, but also feel free to like, just ask as we're going.
Okay, sounds good.
Perfect.
So I lead customer operations for GrowthX, which means I manage really like the process and people that go into creating our content.
So GrowthX has an internal platform that just launched a month ago that leverages AI throughout a few checkpoints of a workflow to generate content at scale for our clients.

And that means that we have several roles that help create that output and make sure that it's to the client's taste and also that like the flow generally from like a technical perspective and an AI perspective is working.
So this role, technical managing editor, sort of the project management layer between our account director.
Or account strategists and our content managers, which are essentially like the first line of defense that are running the workflows, running a first edit, kind of tweaking inputs.

And then the technical managing editor thinks holistically about making sure that content is meeting style, brand, guidelines, really is not hallucinating, is talking about the, you know, the right pieces about the product and just, you know, leveraging keywords optimized for SEO, things like that.
Ideally, our content and our CMS will also be working on that, but you're sort of the person that is coordinating also with the client on sending that out.
So you're the second level of check and making sure that all those things look good.
And as part of that, you know, this is still a relatively new platform that we're using internally.
So

We're looking for someone who has some experience thinking about AI prompting and can identify the points in a workflow where things break, because through our workflows, there are a couple of human-in-the-loop moments.

We're pulling in context from artifacts that have been generated kind of with humans in AI, like running multiple iterations, and so making sure that we have those right inputs and that we can communicate with engineering on what's going wrong and also, like, help work with the client to optimize what they're looking for in terms of the output.
So, you know, a little bit of technical chops and project, from really, like, project management technical, like, you don't have to know how to code.
And then also just somebody that's comfortable, at least, like, from a quarterbacking perspective, talking to clients on, here's the deliverable.
Well, this is what's now, this is what's next, this is kind of where we are in the project.
Do you have any questions?

No, I think I'm understanding what you're saying is that a lot of the role is related to, like you said, like technical project management, making sure that the AI workflows that you're using work and identifying when there's issues.
And then also the kind of account and customer management side, so like communications, that project management on that side.

And is that, and of course, like quality control throughout, like you mentioned, like CM should be, you know, checking the quality of their work, but then I'm kind of, this role would be like the second line of defense, essentially.
Is that correct?
Exactly.
Perfect.
Okay.
Great.
So why don't you tell me a little bit about yourself?
Yeah, so I've been in content marketing for about six years now and in more generalist brand marketing for a few years before that.
Um, I've been at animals, a content marketing agent.

For a little over three years, and I would say the majority of my content marketing experience has been in B2B marketing, and especially animals working with a bunch of different SaaS and tech companies.

So I'm really comfortable working across different industries, learning about different topics, products, audiences, and having to ramp up very quickly on those just by the nature of working at an agency.
And I have a lot of experience across different types of content production.
So from more, I would say, like traditional long-form content, like blogs, case studies, data reports, white papers, and also kind of more distribution-oriented content as well.
So social media, email.
I've also experimented a little bit in, not like experimented, but done a lot of like LinkedIn thought leadership-related things.
So like interviewing founders or like C-suite level SMEs to, you know, get their insight.
and opinions on their industry or what things that would be for the target audience and producing content based on that.
So I'm really comfortable interviewing subject matter experts across like different levels of the organization and building and maintaining those relationships.
What else to tell you about me?
I think something that I'm really interested in in terms of my career is getting to focus more on that like relationship building and management aspect of working with clients.
That's something where I find a lot of, I think it's, I really enjoy it.
And I also find it to be really helpful just from like a content perspective of like actually getting to understand a client's company and their goals.
Because naturally just being in an agency, we're not spending like 40 hours a week with our clients the way that an in-house person would.
And so this is like, I see it as the best opportunity to really understand what's going on with them, what's going on at the company, at company, what problems or challenges.
Which is they're facing and how we as content marketers can fill in those gaps and help them.
So, yeah, you mentioned that's like a big part of this role.
And that's something that I personally really enjoy in my role and in past roles and something that I'm really looking to grow my skills in and just get more opportunities to do that in the future.
Amazing.
Can you tell me a little bit about your experience working with AI?
Yeah.
So, I mean, of course, I would say I've started using AI tools, like starting with something as basic as ChatGPT, like a little over two years ago when it started being really like widely available.
And starting with like, how can I integrate this into different parts of my day or my workflow to just make them faster?
Sure.
So, I think one of the earliest things I did was just using AI tools for research, which at the time I think required a lot more quality checking and quality control.
Like, as you mentioned, there's a lot of hallucinating that can happen.
lot hallucinating that
So it was definitely more efficient, but not always as efficient as it could have been like it is now, because now we can like, you know, site sources and all of that.

I'm very comfortable building, like I have a lot of experience just like crafting prompts and refining prompts based on the output and building basically like step-by-step workflows that I've used and tested and then was able to share externally or not externally with my team.

Things that could help with, especially when it comes to, you know, SEO focused content production, like related to researching keywords or comparing top ranking pages on the SERP, what are competitors doing that maybe indicates where they're focusing their SEO efforts that might be relevant to our client.
I would say that's kind of where a lot of my focus was in the beginning and then moving more toward actual like AI assisted content production.
So how can we create briefs that are relevant to the client?

But also really comprehensive and useful for the reader, creating outlines, especially when it comes to SEO content, like how do you make sure that our outlines are as comprehensive, and but also better than the top ranking pages.
And of course, just typical AI assisted writing.
So, you know, kind of going step by step to create content without just, you know, typing up maybe like, oh, I want 1000 word article on this topic, can you write it for me?
I've never done that.
Yeah, I am curious to know, I mean, I just don't think it would be very good.
I think it leaves a lot of risk for hallucinations, or just like, kind of like AI slop that I'm sure you've seen.

Yeah, I'm really comfortable building those kind of like step by step workflows where there's opportunities for like human quality checking to make sure that we're not just, I mean, I think AI can be very helpful, especially for content marketers, but I don't think it means that we just remove the human component for I think that is what makes

And maybe like our work, my work, different from a company where they're just hoping that someone can take on content marketing because they have AI tools, which is just, I think, really stressful for that person.
Yeah.
So what platforms did you use when you were generating these workflows?
Mostly ChatGPT and Cloud.
I have a little bit of experience in Aerox, but I haven't built out like massive workflows in Aerox or anything.
Hmm.
Yeah.
And can you give me like a specific example of, you know, a process of a piece of content that you generated and how like you went back and forth?
Yeah.
So I think something that I do most often is related to SEO content.
So the starting point would be, I pull the top, I would say 10 to 20 pages that rank highest for our target keyword and feed those URLs into AI and ask it to...

Analyze all of these pages and create kind of like a comprehensive outline of all the topics that they cover, whether they focus on specific types of information that are related to our specific target audience.
And then that gives me kind of like a really huge outline that I can go through and see, okay, these are the things that these top ranking pages focus on.
Obviously, it's not really realistic or maybe even helpful for readers to just cover everything in a 5,000-word article.
And so that gives me a foundation to choose like, okay, these are the things that are relevant to our readers and also to the client's messaging and product.

So I can kind of pull from there to build an outline that's tailored to the client and their target audience, but also will increase their chances of performing better on the SERP, like keyword rankings and therefore traffic and everything that comes after that.
And from there, that also helps me write briefs.
So creating like a really detailed outline of like this.
is what all the sections should be, this is what you should cover, also including like internal links to incorporate.

That way I find that, you know, having these really detailed briefs, if I'm not able to work on the content and has to be outsourced to a freelancer, I think that really helps with just quality in general, instead of leaving it kind of vague and broad for them to do.
If they're not as familiar with the client as I am, that can just leave them open to maybe not understanding like the topic or the target audience or how to incorporate the client's product into it.

And then from there, now that I have this like really specific brief and outline and have done already some research on my own that I kind of like have in my own mind, but also through AI, I'm able to produce and actually write the article much faster than before when I was, I was like doing all of the same steps and processes manually, literally like manually comparing pages and building outlines.
But now that's a lot faster and honestly, probably more accurate because I'm able to, you know, get more information.
And compare more pages or websites, rather than just me manually typing out these outlines.
Yeah, yeah.
That's a great example.
Thank you so much.
Okay, so thinking about like the client management side, I mean, I'm sure that you supported multiple clients in your work at a given time.
How do you think about prioritization and managing concurrent projects?

Yeah, I think, I mean, it can definitely be tricky at an agency wanting everything to be top priority and every client, maybe it's interesting to see how their perspective is, like, they want everything that we do to be top priority for them.
But if you're managing like five clients a month, you can't always prioritize everything at the same time.

So I think a lot of it comes down to relationship building and building trust with the client so that they know that you are kind of always thinking about them and always prioritizing them, but that's not necessarily reflected back on.
And like my day-to-day work or my overall workload.

And so there are some clients that I find in my experience where they're a little more maybe like relaxed or they're more trusting of knowing that they will receive deliverables or that they will receive things in due time.

And so those clients I'm able to maybe prioritize a little less, not be as like hyper aware of what's going on with them versus other clients that maybe they have more, maybe they have a content or marketing team already.
So they have people who have the time to, you know, work closely with the agency.

Then those are the people that I tend to prioritize more in communications or making sure that they're up to date on what we're working on or if there's anything new that we're developing at the company.
So I would say that's typically how I approach prioritization.
And of course, trying to, you know, different projects or deliverables or also internally, like when we're working on new products or services at the company, trying to balance that as well.
Thank you.
Okay.
Yeah.
How do you go about building that trust?
I think it's really helpful if I can be involved in the client as early as possible.
I think sometimes there have been instances where I'm kind of moved to an account where I haven't built a relationship as early as possible.
And it can be harder to build that trust with the client, especially if I'm a new person and maybe someone moved to a different client and they're kind of like starting over branding with me.
And so I think the biggest thing, especially when it comes to content marketing, is proving quality and trust through the deliverables that we share.
So like making sure, especially if it's an existing client, making sure that the quality of whatever deliverables I'm working on are just as good as what they were before.
And really trying to make sure I'm up to date and aware of like the context of the account based on, you know, our previous notes, call recordings, things like that.
So I'm not asking.
Like, too many dumb questions.
Like, of course, I think it's, I will always ask questions if I need more information.
But if it's something that the client has covered before with the previous person, I don't want to be that person who's asking these questions and making them repeat themselves.

And when it comes to new clients, I think it's really important to set the tone of, like, strong communication, like being really open to collaboration, which I find to be really helpful working with clients.
I think, of course, it is important to, you know, present yourself as, like, an expert and the one consulting them on what they should do with their content marketing.
But I find that people really just want, like, the opportunity to share their ideas and maybe present some things they've been thinking about, even if we're not going to act on them and do them.
So I think that really helps with fostering more of a collaborative relationship, rather than just, you hired us, you pay us money, we deliver this to you.
And it's more like a vendor, customer type of relief.

Which I think when inevitably there are challenges or maybe there's like a churn risk or something, it can be harder to work against that or resolve those issues if you don't have that collaboration already established.
Yeah, I mean, we think we talk a lot internally about like a best hire mindset.
So you want the client to think about you as like an internal, part of their internal team, yet they like hired and couldn't live without.
Yeah, that makes sense.
Rather than the vendor.

Yeah, yeah, I like the way that you phrase that, like a best hire mindset, because that is something I try to do in my work is even if I'm not, I obviously don't have the time to work full time for all my clients.
It's just not realistic, but try to be as aware and embedded into what's going on in their company so that it feels like we're able to provide like fill in those gaps like you described.
Yeah, that's a great answer.
Um, okay, well, you covered a lot in those two answers.
So I sometimes have to ask follow up questions, but I feel like I got really a good sense from those.
So what do you have for me?
Yeah.
So in my previous conversation with Marilia, we talked a little bit about like team structure.
And I had some other questions that she said would be better answered by you, like you have more insight into the role and to the team in general.
And so I think my first question would be, how would you measure the success for the technical managing editor?
Like what would they need to do or prove or share that would show that they're doing really well at their job at three months or six months?
Yeah.
So I think in the last few weeks, really, we have thought a lot about, um, calibrating the, um, or standardizing what we think out.

What should be for this role, because we hired a couple of people, there are maybe like 10 of these, think today, 10 technical managing editors, but this is, we've like really built a new rubric, and we're, you know, that we're bringing everybody up to, thinking about the number of clients that you can manage based on the, like, content in their scope of work.
So the number of deliverables in their scope of work.

And that means like, not just like quality content, but also being able, like, quality content at scale comes from running the workflows and being able to ask engineering for what you need, and really express the ways that you need the workflows and the inputs to be better.
And so I think like, it's a capacity question in terms of like, how many clients are you managing and how much out?

What output are you generating, but it's, you know, like, we have in the past hired more content managers to help generate, but now that we have our own internal platform, we really want to calibrate on improving the platform, eventually, it may be an, like, it may be its own SaaS that we sell access to, in addition to, like, our services arm.
So, like, this technical managing editor is really, like, internally are, like, the driver of the UX and the experience and evolution of the product.

And so the only way the product gets better is if you use it, and then, you know, if you use it correctly, we do see that there are managing editors that can manage, like, five clients and put out, you know, 50, 60 pieces of content a week with their, I mean, they have the content managers that are generating content in their manager.

That's a function of knowing how to refine the inputs and how to think about some of the things that you talked about, making sure that the calibration and the keywords are really right and you have all of the company context input so that the AI can take all of that into account when it's actually generating.
And we do have, we have like our article brief generator, then there's like an outline step where you check the outline and all of those things.
But, you know, if you're running that brainlessly and not thinking about the inputs at each step, you're not going to get good output and then it takes an extra two hours to edit that article.
Yeah, I'm familiar.
Yeah, I think basically as soon as projects started becoming a thing in Quad and Jet GPT, that is where I was running all like different projects for each client.
Because before I would have to manually put like all the files and all the context and custom instructions into like a new chat.
So it's good to know that input is very important.
It's for producing quality output.
It just makes sense.
Yeah.
Yeah, so it's basically, like, capacity and number of output as a function of, like, using the tool correctly.
Okay.
Yeah.
That makes sense.
And I'm curious to know, you mentioned that you built, like, an internal platform for content production.
So does that mean, like, I'm curious, like, what tools or platforms are powering all these workflows?
Like, are you using, I don't know, AROPs?
Or have you built something internally that's completely separate, but maybe using, you know, these large language models?
Or I'm curious to know how that works.
Yeah.
So we start, like, GrowthX started because Marcel, the CEO, did build a bunch of these workflows in AROPs.
Okay.
He um, he started by selling, like, he was like, I'll sell access to a workshop.
Yeah.
of, like, how you can replicate my work.
And then everybody came to the workshop and was like, this is really hard.

You, like, yes, it's in your office, but you're going to, like, image generation and putting things in Claude and then putting things back through ChatGPT and then going into Manus and, like, going into Perplexity and pulling all these different pieces at different stages because each model is, like, better at different parts of the workflow.
And so then people were like, can we just pay you to do this?
Right.
So he built the initial workflows for our first clients in AirOps, but just we wanted to control kind of the direction of the product and what we were building.
And so the workflows are reminiscent of the way it works in AirOps in terms of, like, building or, like, Zapier.
You know, you're just building different pieces, different automations.
But everything, all of that is built on our own code base where we're...
Like writing the prompts that go into, and then we can specify the model that we want to go to.
So like Cloud, ChatGPT, whatever we want it to go to at that stage.
that's cool.
And I'll pull back into our workflows.
Okay, that's cool.
Interesting.
Okay, and then I know we're kind of at the end of our time, so I just want be mindful.
I'm just wondering, what's the longer-term vision for growthx?
Like especially when it comes to, you know, the rise of like AI search and AI search visibility, like answer engine optimization.
I'm wondering, is that part of the long-term vision for the company?
Or what else is involved like a year from now?
I know it's still a pretty new company, so things like, Marilee and I talked about that.
But I'm just curious if you have more insights on that you can share with me.
Yeah, so we're calling it GEO right now, like generative engine optimization.
I think there are like a couple of acronyms.

Our head of growth is thinking about that a lot and is like actually I lead a meeting with like our account directors in about an hour and he's gonna like talk to us about like what's changed and what's happening and how we optimize for that because I think our stance on like AI search is still that like the content that you're putting out, the freshness of that content is incredibly important, but you have to still own the have the authority on a con on like a con a keyword or like piece of context in order to rank and like people still have to buy the thing and know how to buy the thing and so you still need content to like get people to through your funnel right yeah like the AI has to have something to search to be able to give you those answers so we're thinking a lot about that and like I think our strategies are like
Evolving, we work really closely with Scrunch, which basically does a lot of like prompt tracking.
Yeah.
And so like we basically have a Scrunch account for every client.
Okay.

And then, yeah, in terms of like the business model, so we will eventually make the platform external facing, but services itself, mean, you work for an agency, you know, like, even if you make the platform external, there are still people that want to pay you to do that piece of work.
And the like back and forth and human in the loop that it takes, like our stance is that that will always be valuable.
And that's a much bigger bucket of like TAM than, than SaaS itself.
Okay.
Yeah.
That kind of actually, oh my God, I had another question that came up.
Oh, I'm wondering how performance reporting works.
Like, is it this role that's doing performance reporting or is that content managers or how does that work for your clients?
Yeah, so it's.
It's changing.
We just hired somebody that's, like, running, sort of, like, publishing into the CMSs and is also pulling analytics.
Right now we're doing it in Looker dashboards eventually and, like, pulling, like, GSC and, like, the work from the LLM, like, Scrunch all into a Looker dash.
Eventually, so this role would not be responsible for, like, putting all of that together.
Eventually, that will live in our platform, but I don't know when.
Okay, so just so I'm kind of understanding the role, like you said, it's mostly, like, quality control for content production, also building the workflows themselves.
So in terms of, like, strategy or, like, why we're producing content or building content roadmaps, is that part of this role as well or is that, does that live somewhere else?
Yeah, I mean, so we do have, like, these account directors are high-level strategists, but they're, I think, this is really, like...
A close partnership.
The most successful pods are the managing editor is owning, like really owning the client relationship, making recommendations at different stages based on performance.
Like you're not pulling the reports, but you should always like be aware.
We have access.
Yeah.
Have access.
What are our recommendations?
Like none of that stuff is a secret.
And so like, if you're good at driving that, certainly that like, that can be part of the role.
We have some people in this role who are like, I really like, I'm so into the content generation and the technical piece and not so in.
So like, that's why we're both sides of it.
But there's like an opportunity for you to own more of that strategic piece as you go.
And then obviously, like, that's also a chance to level up over time.
If we're nice to grow.
Yeah.
No, that's definitely, I think a goal in my career is to continue growing in strategy.
And of course, that involves like reporting.
Analytics and just having that context of what's happening in the client's company, rather than just production.
Okay, cool.
Well, I want to be mindful over time.
So I think I'm good on questions.
But yeah.
Yeah, thank you for your time, Natalie.
was great to talk to you.
All right.
Thank you so much.
I really appreciate it.
And getting to meet you too.
Have a good day.
Thanks, you too.
Bye.
